data.epi_content:
"Hey, Vivian was supposed to send us through.

Would you mind clearing the way for us?"

Fortunately, Vivian was nowhere to be seen.

She probably thought it was fine as long as she didn't personally get involved.

Was she trying to keep her promise in this roundabout way, or had someone else forced this situation against her will?

Whatever the case, I'd been betrayed as expected.

They were grinding their teeth, faces contorted with anger, as they began pointing those primitive cold weapons at us.

"It was Vivian's idea to let these devil-like murderers go free, not ours."

"Just a male prostitute who barks or dies at Vivian's command."

"Rich words coming from a black magic whore."

This was never going to be a friendly conversation.

I stepped back slightly and whispered so only Evan could hear.

"We're going to run. Even if we have to fight, the first priority is to escape, got it?"

Evan nodded.

Together, Evan and I raised countless skeletons from the ground and had them scatter putrid flesh chunks all around.

In an instant, a foul stench spread, creating something resembling a horrific massacre scene.

Ivan kept scratching his nose with his front paw, bothered by the smell.

I placed a small wind barrier over his nose, and he finally made a satisfied growling sound.

Just as the tension seemed to ease slightly, the gleaming blade of a sword approached me, causing my body to stiffen.

Anyone would be terrified when a bipedal beast standing easily 190cm tall comes charging with a sword.

The Black in front, slicing through numerous skeletons, rushes toward me to cut off my head.

Two skilled knights, one mage, and one human who could use both sword and magic reasonably well.

Four of them—we should be able to get past them and escape somehow.

"Evan, at best I can only hold off this Black one. If you see an opportunity while fighting the other three, crush this with your fingers."

With those words, I handed Evan a gem similar to the one he'd given me before, telling me to break it if he needed help.

Evan stared at it for a while, then nodded and separated from me to fight the other three.

I'm glad I didn't go in that direction.

I'd probably be cut in half instantly by those spells and flashing swords.

"Even though I've fallen from nobility to become a terrible black mage, and I'm weak, I won't lose to a mere beast."

He instantly cut down five skeletons, let out a barbaric roar, raised his sword high, and charged at me.

I pulled out my pistol, aimed it at him, and frantically pulled the trigger.

The bullets fired and hit their mark.

But they merely grazed his skin, creating tiny wounds with no significant effect.

That's fine.

The distance is still great.

If he approaches, I can fall back; if he retreats, I can advance.

He roars like a lion and charges at me with all his might, but it's useless.

The field of rotting corpses, the stench of dirty water flowing nearby, and the tiny cursed bone dust—ground so fine it could be called bone powder—floating in the air all work to hinder him.

"I can't kill you, I don't have the strength for that.

But you can't kill me either, you don't have the ability."

Suddenly, a hand shoots out from the corpse-filled mud and begins pulling at the Black.

I hadn't expected that—do all those who die without receiving a priest's last rites harbor such resentment toward the living?

"So, let's just part ways.
data.epi_content2:
We've both seen plenty of each other, haven't we?

Or would you rather step into that mud, hold your breath to avoid inhaling bone dust, and keep trying to cut my throat while I keep running away—repeating this futile exercise forever?"

Glancing over at Evan, his situation appeared similar.

The number of skeletons is practically infinite, especially in such a confined space.

Slave traders used to say this often:

They'd never seen a civilized tribe among Black people, nor even an individual with exceptional behavior or thinking abilities.

Perhaps they're too stupid to understand social compromise and promises.

The only ones capable of conversation probably have mixed blood.

I want to escape soon.

I don't want to stay in this unpleasant place any longer.

"I've seen plenty of your kind.

Dirty, obscene, and vile humans who don't bat an eye when an acquaintance dies if they weren't close.

That's why you commit such cruel acts without hesitation.

Let the dead rest in peace."

"Yes, you don't have the strength to charge at me now, and you need to replenish your energy.

What an interesting conversation. If you charge, I'll just run away again, haha."

This Black seems to have a rather broad definition of "human," considering he doesn't see himself as one.

"Would telling you this story make me a hero in your eyes?

All those armored skeletons with swords are knights who hanged farmers for resisting when their crops were taken, knowing they would starve to death. Called them traitors.

Those with spears and leather armor are deserters who enjoyed eating human flesh.

As for the unarmed skeletons, well, there are too many stories to tell."

I don't want to explain this out loud.

I don't want to justify myself to that Black.

Yes, he lacks the power to impose his morality on me.

If it were Vivian here, I wouldn't be a pitiful victim but a disgusting, filthy murderer, or yes, a crippled witch.

Even now, what are they trying to do by pursuing someone who just wants to live quietly in a corner?

"......"

"Look at how stupid you are, can't even respond."

Does he really want to be like that?

His feet sinking into the mud, flesh sticking to him with every movement, foul odors rising all around.

"You probably delegate all the dirty work to your subordinates. Like these guys."

Simultaneously, skeletons with shiny armor and swords approach the Black with clattering sounds.

He looks momentarily perplexed, then decides to stop talking to me and resumes smashing skeletons with renewed vigor.

This is suffocating.

I just want to smoke tobacco and drink coffee somewhere with fresh air and nice scenery.

Evan must have felt the same frustration because he gathered water from the underground waterway—which could just as well be called blood or sewage—heated it intensely, and burst it open.

The smell of blood and all kinds of filth grew even stronger, filling the space.

At least it created some fog.

I stepped into the water and began walking, like a man without principles who resurrects three days after death.

Ivan was afraid of water, and despite my spells, he refused to step in, so I just carried him.

What a useless dog.

Anyway, Evan approached me.

We held hands and began walking forward as if dancing a waltz.

"I've been checking around, but Vivian didn't come. Seems like only these guys came to stop us."
data.epi_content3:
"That makes escaping much easier, right?"

"Yes."

"So what's the plan?"

In response, Evan silently rolled something round in his right hand for a while before speaking.

"If Vivian were here, she wouldn't have given me the chance to create something like this—she would have just frantically cast spells."

Evan gathered that round thing until it was about the size of a boy's head, then tied it off like the end of a balloon and threw it into the air.

It floated slowly before exploding, sending blood-like, already rotting matter in all directions.

I looked at Evan with my nose pinched between my thumb and index finger, giving him a sulky glare as if to say he should have warned me.

Evan grinned and made an excuse.

"It might not be clean, but it's effective."

Since I couldn't see ahead either, I hugged Ivan tightly, grabbed the back of Evan's clothes, and followed him closely.

Soon after leaving the underground waterway, I splashed some water over myself and climbed onto Ivan's back.

"Where should we go now?"

Evan asked.

Since I hadn't really thought of a specific place, I gave a vague answer.

Well, the cabin where I was holed up for a year waiting for Evan might be nice.

But it seems too small for several people to live in.

It felt cramped even when I lived there alone.

"I don't know. Somewhere with good air and clean water would be enough."

"What's that about? You're not an old man yet."

"You're the old man, Evan. You've lived much longer than me."

"That's true."

We fell silent for a while.

Was it because of a sense of emptiness and futility?

Or was it some nameless, overwhelming emotion from finally gaining freedom?

"Was I really worth all this?"

"Yes."

Evan answered as if it were obvious.

"Well, thank you for that. Let's get going."

Is this self-loathing—for using countless others' suffering just for my own sake—only surfacing now that it's over?

A staleness lingered in my mouth.

I don't regret it.

I can't afford to be that kind of person.

My thoughts have wandered.

Come to think of it, those idiots are still chasing us from behind.

"Let's go somewhere without people, wherever that may be."

Let's go somewhere without people to point fingers at me.

As always, I swallowed the last words, lit my tobacco, clenched it between my teeth, and let the smoke carry them away.
